activities.markdown:
# Clave: The Rhythmic DNA of Latin Music

The **clave** is a 2-bar rhythmic pattern that serves as the foundational skeleton of virtually all Afro-Cuban and Latin music. Every other instrument plays in relationship to the clave — either reinforcing it, playing against it, or complementing it.

**The two main claves:**

**Son Clave (3-2):** Three hits in bar 1, two hits in bar 2
- Bar 1: beat 1, beat 2-and, beat 4
- Bar 2: beat 2, beat 3

**Son Clave (2-3):** The reverse — 2 hits first, 3 hits second

**Rumba Clave:** Similar to son clave but the 3rd hit of the '3-side' is a 16th note later. This gives rumba its distinct, more 'pulled' feel.

**Critical concept:** You can't 'flip' the clave mid-song accidentally. If you're playing on the 3-side of the clave and you add an extra bar, you've 'flipped' the clave — catastrophic in traditional Latin music.

activities.title: Son Clave 3-2 (Bar 1: 3-side)
activities.description: The three-hit side of the son clave. Three hits land on beat 1, the 'and' of beat 2, and beat 4. This pattern has a forward-leaning, anticipatory feel.

activities.title: Son Clave 3-2 (Bar 2: 2-side)
activities.description: The two-hit side of the son clave. Two hits on beats 2 and 3. The comparative emptiness of this bar creates tension that resolves when the 3-side returns.

activities.question: In a 3-2 son clave, how many hits are in the first bar?
activities.explanation: In a 3-2 son clave, bar 1 has 3 hits (landing on beat 1, the and of 2, and beat 4), and bar 2 has 2 hits (landing on beats 2 and 3).
